cuisine: Western / International (widely adopted in Singapore and Southeast Asia)
region_of_origin: Europe (French origin); widely adopted in Singapore and Southeast Asia

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic croquette class; no chicken-specific croquette subclass exists. Mapped to nearest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Croquettes — breaded and fried meat-based product
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers breaded/fried meat croquettes; chicken-specific subcode not available in public FoodEx2 browser. Mapped to nearest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for chicken croquette found in public SNOMED CT browser.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single chicken croquette piece weighs approximately 60 g and provides around 160 kcal, making it a common snack or side-dish portion.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Croquette — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F11020015 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this source. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for chicken croquette (closest analogue) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on breaded potato-based fried snack (GI ~65)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken croquette in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: breaded and fried potato-based snack/croquette with refined breadcrumb coating. Carbohydrate content is 17.1 g/100g; GL per 60 g serving = (65 × 17.1 × 0.60) / 100 = 6.7. Estimation is consistent with the high-GI nature of refined breadcrumbs and mashed potato filling.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 310 mg/100 g with zero dietary fibre and negligible potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit chicken croquette to one piece per meal, avoid high-sodium dipping sauces such as soy sauce or ketchup, and request baked or air-fried preparation to reduce trans fat, which contributes to arterial stiffness.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (65) refined carbohydrate at 17.1 g/100 g with zero dietary fibre, producing rapid postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict chicken croquette to one piece per sitting, pair it with high-fibre non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response, and avoid sugary dipping sauces that further elevate postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (17.6 g/100 g) including saturated fat (~4.2 g) and trans fat (~0.4 g) from deep-frying, combined with elevated sodium (310 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit deep-fried chicken croquettes due to the combined burden of saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium; substituting with a baked or air-fried version and reducing portion size to one piece is strongly advised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and trans fat (~0.4 g/100 g), both of which are pro-inflammatory; zero anti-inflammatory nutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried chicken croquettes as AGE formation and trans fat content worsen joint inflammation; a baked version with anti-inflammatory herbs is a preferable alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken croquette is a breaded and deep-fried patty filled with a mixture of minced chicken, mashed potato, and vegetables. The exterior is coated in fine breadcrumbs that form a crisp, golden crust upon deep-frying, while the interior remains soft and savoury. The dish is widely consumed in Singapore as a snack, side dish, or light meal component and is commonly found in hawker centres, school canteens, and fast-food outlets. Known in Chinese as 鸡肉可乐饼, in Malay as Kroket Ayam, in Tamil as கோழி கிரோக்கெட், and in Hindi as चिकन क्रोकेट, it is a popular item across Singapore's multicultural food landscape. Per 100 g, it provides 267 kcal, 10.4 g protein, 17.6 g total fat, and 17.1 g carbohydrate. Sodium is 310 mg/100 g, which is moderate to high. Dietary fibre is 0 g, meaning the food offers no fibre benefit. The deep-frying process introduces saturated fat (~4.2 g/100 g) and trans fat (~0.4 g/100 g), both of which are clinically significant for cardiovascular and inflammatory conditions. The glycaemic index is estimated at 65 (medium), driven by the refined breadcrumb coating and mashed potato filling. This food is not suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis without strict portion control and preparation modification. Halal-certified versions are widely available in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a chicken croquette?
Is chicken croquette high in sodium?
What is the GI of chicken croquette?
How much fat is in a chicken croquette?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F11020015, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central closest analogue and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous food with full documentation. All values are internally consistent. Local-language names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are standard transliterations/translations of the dish name, consistent with Singapore's multilingual food naming conventions.
notes: sugar_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB source — the savoury filling and breadcrumb coating contain no added sugar and the potato starch is reported as 0 g sugar by HPB. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB source — refined breadcrumbs and processed potato filling contain negligible fibre below detection threshold. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific HPB chicken croquette entry are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. trans_fat_g set to 0.4 g/100 g: estimated from deep-frying process literature and USDA analogue data; not directly reported by HPB. potassium_mg set to 220 mg/100 g: estimated from USDA FoodData Central closest analogue for chicken-potato croquette; not directly reported by HPB. Input dietary_tags incorrectly listed vegetarian and plant_based — corrected to false as this product contains chicken. Occasion tag breakfast retained as culturally plausible in Singapore context but not primary. GI is an analogous estimate; direct measurement not available in University of Sydney GI Database for this specific product. Multilingual local names added in iteration 3 to satisfy G6 and Q1/Q8: Chinese (鸡肉可乐饼 / 雞肉可樂餅), Malay (Kroket Ayam), Tamil (கோழி கிரோக்கெட்), Hindi (चिकन क्रोकेट). Although the dish has Western/French origins, it is widely consumed across Singapore's multicultural population and the validator requires Singaporean multilingual coverage given the HPB source and Singapore food context.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is Chicken Croquette — precise canonical English name. Local-language names now present for Chinese (鸡肉可乐饼 / 雞肉可樂餅), Malay (Kroket Ayam), Tamil (கோழி கிரோக்கெட்), and Hindi (चिकन क्रोकेट) to satisfy G6 multilingual compliance as required by the validator for this HPB Singapore-sourced record.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal 267.0, protein_g 10.4, fat_g 17.6, carbohydrate_g 17.1 — all present, numeric, and sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (reviewed, confidence 0.95). nutrient_source fully documented.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB), sodium_mg 310.0 (HPB), saturated_fat_g 4.2 (USDA analogue, documented), sugar_g 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB). All four required secondary fields present and numeric.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index 65 (medium), gi_category medium, glycemic_load_per_serving 6.7, gi_source documented as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021. Estimation basis fully documented.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn croquette parent class mapped with closest_parent match and gap note. FoodEx2 A0CJW breaded/fried meat croquette mapped with closest_parent match and gap note. SNOMED CT null with gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well above 200-character minimum. Covers food identity, preparation, texture, cultural context including multilingual names, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease. Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name Type 2 Diabetes used correctly; machine key type2_diabetes retained.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases array contains all English variants plus Chinese (鸡肉可乐饼, 雞肉可樂餅), Malay (Kroket Ayam, kroket ayam, ayam kroket), Tamil (கோழி கிரோக்கெட்), and Hindi (चिकन क்रோக்கெட்) entries. All aliases_by_language string values verified present verbatim in flat aliases array. Mandatory language coverage satisfied for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as required by validator feedback.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft produced with core macros, GI, ontology, and health context.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'. Attempted to argue Western cuisine exemption from G6 Singaporean multilingual rule.

Iteration 3: Added mandatory local-language names for Chinese (鸡肉可乐饼 / 雞肉可樂餅), Malay (Kroket Ayam), Tamil (கோழி கிரோக்கெட்), and Hindi (चिकन क्रोकेट) to local_names section. Added all local-language forms to aliases flat array and aliases_by_language structured object. Updated aliases_by_language chinese, malay, tamil, and hindi arrays with corresponding entries. Verified all aliases_by_language string values appear verbatim in flat aliases array. This resolves G6 hard gate failure and Q1/Q8 failures identified in iteration 2 validator feedback.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Chicken Croquette (F11020015) passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 in iteration 3. The critical fix in this iteration was adding mandatory multilingual local names and aliases for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as required by the validator for this HPB Singapore-sourced record. Core macros are sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (reviewed, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat and potassium are estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogue with full documentation. Trans fat is estimated from deep-frying literature. GI is an analogous estimate (65, medium) from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 with GL calculated for a 60 g serving. All three mandatory health conditions are present with specific clinical guidance. Ontology uses closest_parent matches with gap notes. All -1 sentinel values are documented in notes. Zero-value macros (sugar, dietary fibre) are justified as confirmed zero per HPB. No forbidden sources used.
